prompt: Share an untranslatable word or idiom from any language you know about — something that doesn't have a clean English equivalent — and explain what it captures.
response:
One untranslatable word that comes to mind is "tsundoku" from Japanese. It's a term that describes a condition where you buy so many books that they start piling up and you don't know what to do with them anymore. The word even has its origins in the word "doku," meaning to read, and "tsun," meaning to pile up or stack.

This word captures a very specific sense of book lover's guilt or overwhelm. The English equivalent might be "book hoarder," but that doesn't quite capture the regret that comes with it. When you say you have tsundoku, you're not just saying you have a lot of unread books, you're also expressing your guilt and maybe a bit of sadness about your situation. It's almost like having buyer's remorse, but on a larger scale.

The word tsundoku speaks to the common experience of buying more books than you realistically have time to read. It's a condition that has become more prevalent in our digital age, where it's so easy to buy ebooks with a few swipes of our fingers. This adds an interesting layer to the concept - not just physical clutter, but also the clutter in our digital lives.
